itineraries.title: Bangladesh Grand Tour: Ganges Delta, Sylhet Tea Gardens & Cox's Bazar
itineraries.subtitle: From Dhaka's Mughal palaces to the winding canals of the Ganges Delta, from Sylhet's colonial tea estates to the endless beaches of Cox's Bazar: 7 days of striking contrasts through the heart of Bangladesh, journeying between rivers, rice paddies and the Bay of Bengal.
specialties: Malaicherra tea from Bangladesh's oldest estate

map_keywords: Jatiya Sangsad Bhaban National Parliament Louis Kahn Dhaka
Hazrat Shahjalal International Airport DAC Dhaka

visits.text: Kawran Bazar, one of South Asia's largest wholesale markets: founded in the 18th century by a Marwari merchant, it now supplies the entire capital with vegetables, fish and fruit. Over 2,000 shops across 1 km² handle 50 million taka worth of transactions daily. At night, hundreds of trucks unload their cargo while porters weave through the aisles balancing baskets on their heads—a loud, sensory immersion into Dhaka's grassroots economy.

Jatiya Sangsad Bhaban, the National Parliament complex designed by American architect Louis Kahn between 1962 and 1982: nine raw concrete blocks encircle the central octagonal legislative chamber, the entire ensemble set on 200 acres beside an artificial lake. Winner of the 1989 Aga Khan Award for Architecture, it's universally regarded as one of the 20th century's masterpieces. Visit from the outer esplanade and lakeside paths—interior access is restricted.

activities.text: Arrival at Hazrat Shahjalal International Airport (DAC) and visa-on-arrival formalities (available for most European nationalities, expect $50 USD).
Purchase an eSIM or local Grameenphone SIM at the Arrivals hall—4G network covering the entire country, essential for maps and transportation.
Prepaid taxi or Uber transfer to the city center (Paltan/Tejgaon)—expect 45 to 90 minutes depending on Dhaka's notorious traffic.

map_keywords: Ahsan Manzil Pink Palace Dhaka museum Nawab Buriganga
Lalbagh Fort Mughal 17th century Old Dhaka

visits.text: Baitul Mukarram, Bangladesh's National Mosque: a modernist white marble structure inspired by the Ka'aba in Mecca, built from 1960 onward in a cubic style. Its grand prayer hall can accommodate up to 40,000 worshippers. Non-Muslims may visit outside prayer times. The bustle of religious-goods markets surrounding the building is especially vibrant.

Ahsan Manzil, the Pink Palace of the Nawabs of Dhaka on the banks of the Buriganga: built between 1859 and 1872 in an Indo-Saracenic style blending Mughal and European influences, this pink-domed palace now houses 23 galleries chronicling the opulent life of Bengali aristocracy under the British Raj—period furniture, portraits and personal belongings of the Nawabs. Managed by the National Museum. Closed Thursdays. Paid entry (500 Tk for foreigners).

Lalbagh Fort, an unfinished 17th-century Mughal fortress in Old Dhaka: begun in 1678 by Prince Muhammad Azam, son of Aurangzeb, and abandoned after the death of Pari Bibi, daughter of Governor Shaista Khan. Spread over 18 acres, the complex unites Pari Bibi's marble and basalt mausoleum, the still-active three-domed mosque, and the Diwan-i-Aam (audience hall), all linked by Persian-style gardens. On-site museum of Mughal artifacts.

Dhakeshwari Temple, Dhaka's principal Hindu tutelary deity: according to tradition, built in the 12th century under the Sena dynasty—it's Bangladesh's most important Hindu sanctuary, whose very name gave the city its own. Lively compound, fragrant with incense and steeped in devotion. Free access.

activities.text: Travel by CNG (natural-gas tuk-tuk) or rickshaw between Old Dhaka sites—iconic mode of transport; negotiate the fare before boarding.

map_keywords: Shah Jalal Dargah shrine Sylhet Sufi pilgrimage Bangladesh
Malnicherra Tea Estate oldest tea garden Bangladesh Sylhet
Sylhet Osmani International Airport ZYL Bangladesh

visits.text: Shah Jalal Dargah, Bangladesh's largest pilgrimage site: the tomb of 14th-century Sufi saint Shah Jalal, who introduced Islam to the Sylhet region in 1303 after defeating Hindu chieftain Gour Govinda, draws thousands of faithful daily. The complex comprises four mosques, a mausoleum draped in brocade and illuminated by candles at dusk, and two ponds of sacred catfish—according to legend, the reincarnation of Govinda's defeated soldiers. Non-Muslims are admitted to the compound, barefoot from the East Dargah Gate, but not inside the main mosque.

Malnicherra Tea Estate, the subcontinent's oldest tea plantation: founded in 1849 by Lord Hudson across 1,500 acres along the road to Sylhet airport, it's Bangladesh's first commercial tea operation. You can wander among the rows of pruned tea bushes, watch pickers at work, and stroll shaded avenues between the plants. Orange and rubber trees punctuate the estate. Permission from private managers recommended before entering.

activities.text: Domestic flight from Dhaka (DAC) to Sylhet (ZYL)—approximately 45 minutes flight time. Multiple daily connections (Biman, US-Bangla, Novoair).
Evening stroll through the Zindabazar shopping district—Sylhet's bustling artery, with its stalls of spices, paan (betel leaf) and local textiles.

map_keywords: Ahsan Manzil Pink Palace Dhaka museum Nawab Buriganga
Lalbagh Fort Mughal 17th century Old Dhaka
Sadarghat ferry terminal Buriganga river Dhaka

visits.text: Baitul Mukarram, Bangladesh's National Mosque: a modernist white marble structure inspired by the Ka'aba in Mecca, built from 1960 onward in a cubic style. Its grand prayer hall can accommodate up to 40,000 worshippers. Non-Muslims may visit outside prayer times. The bustle of religious-goods markets surrounding the building is especially vibrant.

Ahsan Manzil, the Pink Palace of the Nawabs of Dhaka on the banks of the Buriganga: built between 1859 and 1872 in an Indo-Saracenic style blending Mughal and European influences, this pink-domed palace now houses 23 galleries chronicling the opulent life of Bengali aristocracy under the British Raj—period furniture, portraits and personal belongings of the Nawabs. Managed by the National Museum. Closed Thursdays. Paid entry (500 Tk for foreigners).

Lalbagh Fort, an unfinished 17th-century Mughal fortress in Old Dhaka: begun in 1678 by Prince Muhammad Azam, son of Aurangzeb, and abandoned after the death of Pari Bibi, daughter of Governor Shaista Khan. Spread over 18 acres, the complex unites Pari Bibi's marble and basalt mausoleum, the still-active three-domed mosque, and the Diwan-i-Aam (audience hall), all linked by Persian-style gardens. On-site museum of Mughal artifacts.

Dhakeshwari Temple, Dhaka's principal Hindu tutelary deity: according to tradition, built in the 12th century under the Sena dynasty—it's Bangladesh's most important Hindu sanctuary, whose very name gave the city its own. Lively compound, fragrant with incense and steeped in devotion. Free access.

activities.text: Return flight Sylhet (ZYL) → Dhaka (DAC) in the morning—45 minutes. Transfer from airport to Old Dhaka (~45 min depending on traffic).
Travel by CNG (natural-gas tuk-tuk) or rickshaw between Old Dhaka sites—the city's iconic mode of transport; negotiate the fare before boarding.

Evening boarding at Sadarghat and VIP cabin check-in on a night launch to Barishal. The Sadarghat river terminal is South Asia's largest: every evening, dozens of multi-deck boats embark thousands of passengers for the delta. Typical departure between 7pm and 9pm, arrival at dawn.

map_keywords: Baitul Aman Mosque Guthia Barisal 20 domes

visits.text: Baitul Aman Mosque (Guthia Mosque), 20 km from Barishal in Wazirpur upazila: inaugurated in 2006, this private 14-acre complex on the banks of a canal unites 20 domes and a 58-meter minaret—Bangladesh's tallest—surrounded by three lakes whose waters reflect the entire edifice. Architecture inspired by great Ottoman and Middle Eastern mosques, with Quranic inscriptions in calligraphy on imported marble. On-site garden and orphanage. Free entry.

Pirogue excursion on the canals of Swarupkathi (35 km south of Barishal): this delta town is crisscrossed by a network of waterways that locals still navigate daily. Swarupkathi is also known for its woodworkers—cabinetmakers and sculptors working with jackfruit and local teak. In season (May–August), pirogues laden with watermelons descend the canals from upstream villages, an emblematic image of the Bengali delta.

activities.text: Sunrise arrival of the launch on the Barishal delta—the brown-golden waters of the Kirtankhola come alive at dawn with fishermen's pirogues and boats loaded with vegetables.
Barishal → Swarupkathi road trip (35 km, ~45 min) for the pirogue excursion, then return to Barishal for the night.

map_keywords: Laboni Beach Cox's Bazar longest sea beach Bangladesh
Inani Beach coral rocks Cox's Bazar Bangladesh
Burmese Market Cox's Bazar handicraft spices
Marine Drive Road Cox's Bazar beach Bangladesh

visits.text: Laboni Point, Cox's Bazar's tourist epicenter: first access to the world's longest uninterrupted beach—120 km of golden sand along the Bay of Bengal. Shell sellers and grilled-seafood vendors, horsemen offering beach rides, souvenir markets at the entrance. The finest hour is sunset, when sampan fishing boats return to port, black silhouettes against the orange sea.

Inani Beach, 25 km south of Cox's Bazar along the Marine Drive: a wilder, less-frequented stretch of beach, distinctive for its black and green coral rock formations surfacing at low tide across 18 km. The water is clearer than at Laboni and the setting more pristine. The sole coastal road between Cox's Bazar and Inani hugs the sea the entire distance.

Burmese Market of Cox's Bazar: a market historically run by traders from neighboring Myanmar, where you'll find sarongs, spices, bamboo goods, natural cosmetics and local handicrafts. A vibrant cultural curiosity in the heart of Cox's Bazar bazaar.

activities.text: Domestic flight from Sylhet (ZYL) to Cox's Bazar (CXB)—approximately 1 hour flight time. Several Bangladeshi carriers operate this route.
Sunset walk on the beach: photos of sampan fishing boats moored in rows, raking light on the sea and silhouettes of nets drying in the wind.

map_keywords: Ethnological Museum Agrabad Chittagong Bangladesh tribal Hill Tracts
Chandanpura Masjid Chittagong colorful mosque Bangladesh
Foy's Lake Chittagong colonial British Bangladesh
Patenga Sea Beach Chittagong Karnaphuli port Bangladesh

visits.text: Ethnological Museum of Agrabad: one of Bangladesh's few ethnographic museums, dedicated to the tribal peoples of the Chittagong Hill Tracts—Chakma, Marma, Tripura, Khyang and Bawm. Collections of textiles, jewelry, musical instruments and everyday objects presented by ethnic group, offering essential background before any visit to the hills.

Chandanpura Masjid, an 18th-century mosque with facades adorned with blue-and-white polychrome glazed tiles—an exceedingly rare decorative scheme in Bangladeshi religious architecture, earning it renown among Islamic architecture enthusiasts. Located in Chittagong's old commercial quarter.

Foy's Lake, an artificial lake created by the British in 1924 by excavating the hillside for construction of the Assam-Bengal Railway. Stroll along the wooded shores, rent rowboats, and enjoy views of the hills encircling Chittagong—a green lung in the country's second city.

Patenga Sea Beach, at the mouth of the Karnaphuli, facing the open sea of the Bay of Bengal. View of cargo ships entering Chittagong port—Bangladesh's second export hub. Popular promenade vibe at sunset, frequented mainly by city residents.

activities.text: Cox's Bazar → Chittagong road trip in the morning (150 km, ~2.5 hours on the Highway). Early departure recommended to make the most of the day in Chittagong.
Chittagong → Dhaka return in the evening—domestic flight CGP→DAC (1h, most practical option) or bus (250 km, ~4-5h depending on traffic).

Last-minute shopping at New Market or Bashundhara City Mall—jamdani fabrics (Bengali brocade inscribed on UNESCO intangible heritage list), spices, bamboo handicrafts and nakshi kantha (traditional embroideries). Transfer to Hazrat Shahjalal Airport (DAC) in the evening or next morning.

practical_info.tip: Allow 3 hours before long-haul departures. Dhaka traffic is unpredictable—anticipate accordingly.

seo.h1_title: Bangladesh Grand Tour: Ganges Delta, Sylhet Tea Gardens & Cox's Bazar
seo.intro_paragraph: Dive into Dhaka's pulsing energy, navigate the Ganges Delta's winding waterways, explore Sylhet's emerald tea gardens, and unwind on the world's longest beach at Cox's Bazar. Bangladesh reveals itself one river at a time.
seo.seo_hook: Tea gardens awaken in the morning mist, shuttle boats crisscross the river arteries, endless beaches stretch golden into the horizon. Bangladesh thrums with water and green.
featured_list: Dhaka: Lalbagh Fort and the Nawabs' Pink Palace on the Buriganga
Ganges Delta: overnight launch on the river, Swarupkathi canals by pirogue
Sylhet: the subcontinent's oldest tea garden, established 1849
Shah Jalal Dargah: Bangladesh's largest pilgrimage site
Cox's Bazar: 120 km of golden sand, the world's longest beach
Chittagong: tribal peoples, colorful tiled mosque and colonial-era lake

places_taglines.BD::dhaka: Where Mughal palaces rise above the chaos and the Buriganga pulses with river life
places_taglines.BD::barisal: The Venice of Bangladesh, where a thousand canals lead deep into the Ganges Delta
places_taglines.BD::swarupkathi: Delta village alive with fruit-laden pirogues and master woodcarvers
places_taglines.BD::sylhet: Where emerald tea gardens cascade down hillsides beneath monsoon clouds
places_taglines.BD::coxs-bazar: 120 kilometers of unbroken golden sand along the Bay of Bengal
places_taglines.BD::chattogram: Where tribal heritage meets colonial history on the shores of the Karnaphuli

seo_keywords: Dhaka visit Lalbagh Fort Ahsan Manzil
Jatiya Sangsad Bhaban Louis Kahn architecture

highlights: Jatiya Sangsad Bhaban: Louis Kahn masterpiece, 1989 Aga Khan Award—one of the 20th century's greatest buildings
Overnight launch Dhaka-Barishal: multi-deck river crossing through the Ganges Delta, VIP cabins available
Malnicherra Tea Estate: the subcontinent's oldest tea plantation, founded 1849—5 minutes from Sylhet airport
Shah Jalal Dargah: 14th-century Sufi pilgrimage site, sacred catfish ponds and unique devotional atmosphere
Cox's Bazar: 120 km of uninterrupted beach, Inani Beach with coral formations at low tide
warnings: Dhaka: extremely dense traffic—expect double the travel time during rush hours (7am-10am and 5pm-8pm)
Overnight launch: book a 1st-class (VIP) cabin in advance—open decks are crowded and noisy
Malnicherra Tea Estate: private property—obtain permission at the entrance before venturing into the plantations
Madhabkunda Waterfall: accessible only by road from Sylhet, allow a full day for the round trip
Cox's Bazar in high season (October-January): hotels fill up on weekends—book several days in advance

itineraries.title: Complete Bangladesh: Delta, Sundarbans, Northern Heritage & Chittagong Hill Tracts
itineraries.subtitle: From Dhaka to the Sundarbans mangroves, from Sylhet's hills to Cox's Bazar beaches and Bandarban's tribal peaks—22 days to cross Bangladesh in all its diversity.

visits.text: Baitul Mukarram, Bangladesh's national mosque: a striking modernist structure in white marble inspired by Mecca's Kaaba, built from 1960 onwards. Its vast prayer hall accommodates up to 40,000 worshippers. Non-Muslims may visit outside prayer times. The religious goods markets encircling the building buzz with activity throughout the day.

Sadarghat, South Asia's largest river terminal on the banks of the Buriganga: by late afternoon, dozens of multi-deck launches moor here in organized chaos. Stevedores, street vendors and passengers weave between vessels in a scene untouched by time. The waterfront itself—with its brightly painted wooden boats and rudimentary shipyards—ranks among Bangladesh's most arresting urban spectacles.

Ahsan Manzil, the Pink Palace of Dhaka's Nawabs on the Buriganga riverfront: built between 1859 and 1872 in Indo-Saracenic style blending Mughal and European influences. Its 23 galleries trace the opulent lifestyle of Bengali aristocracy under the British Raj—period furniture, portraits and personal artifacts. Closed Thursdays.

Lalbagh Fort, an unfinished Mughal fortress from 1678: begun by Prince Muhammad Azam, son of Aurangzeb, and abandoned after the death of Pari Bibi. Spanning 18 acres, the complex comprises Pari Bibi's marble and basalt mausoleum, a three-domed mosque still in active use, and the Diwan-i-Aam. Museum of Mughal artifacts on site.

activities.text: Arrival at Hazrat Shahjalal International Airport (DAC). Visa-on-arrival available for most European nationalities. Purchase a local Grameenphone SIM card at the Arrivals hall.
Navigate Old Dhaka's alleyways by CNG auto-rickshaw or cycle rickshaw between sites—the street markets, artisan workshops and riverfront ghats are a spectacle in themselves.

visits.text: Panam Nagar, the ghost town of Sonargaon's Hindu merchants: a single 600-meter street lined with 52 abandoned mansions built between the 19th and early 20th centuries by wealthy muslin traders. Their Indo-Saracenic façades blend European arches, wrought-iron balustrades imported from Calcutta, mosaic tilework and sculpted stucco. The town was deserted after the riots of 1947 and 1964 when the Hindu community fled to India. Listed among the World Monuments Fund's 100 Most Endangered Sites since 2006.

Goaldi Mosque, west of the Sonargaon museum: built under Sultan Hussain Shah in the 16th century, it's one of the best-preserved monuments from the Bengal Sultanate era. Its terracotta brick façade adorned with floral and geometric motifs exemplifies pre-Mughal Bengali style. Still in active use.

Bangladesh Folk Arts and Crafts Foundation, housed in the Bara Sardar Bari from 1901—a zamindar mansion in colonial architecture restored by painter Zainul Abedin in 1975. Two museums: the first devoted to folk arts (textiles, pottery, tools, boat models), the second dedicated to jamdani and nakshi kantha, traditional embroideries inscribed on UNESCO's Intangible Heritage list.

activities.text: Day trip from Dhaka to Sonargaon (28 km, ~1h). Take a CNG from Mograpara Bazar off the Gulistan road.
Walk between Panam Nagar, Goaldi Mosque and the museum—all three sites lie within 15 minutes' walk of each other.

practical_info.tip: Closed Sundays year-round. Opens 10am in summer, 9am in winter.

visits.text: The Rocket Steamer docks at Barisal in early morning. These historic paddle-wheel steamers have plied the delta since 1884—the PS Mahsud, after restoration, resumed service in November 2025 on the Dhaka-Barisal route. A journey through living Bengali railway history.
Guthia Mosque, a few kilometers from the center, rises from the greenery like a miniature Mughal palace. Its white domes and minarets reflect in a perfectly still rectangular pool.
Durgasagar Dighi is the district's largest pond—14 hectares of water ringed by century-old trees. Dug in the 18th century by Raja Shiv Narayan to supply the town, it remains a peaceful promenade.
The Kirtonkhola River quays come alive in late afternoon: dozens of boats laden with rice, coconuts and guavas dock or cast off in unceasing ballet.

activities.text: Rise before dawn to board a motorboat from Banaripara ghat. In the canals, dozens of dugouts loaded with vegetables converge on the floating market—buyers and sellers bargain from boat to boat.

practical_info.tip: Reach Banaripara at dawn (1h from Barisal)

activities.text: A rickshaw tour of Barisal's central bazaar reveals a human-scale city, far from Dhaka's frenzy. Dried fish merchants (shutki) lay out their catch on bamboo racks in full sun.
In evening, settle on the steps of the Kirtonkhola ghat to watch boats light up one by one. Barisal earns its nickname as the Venice of Bengal.

visits.text: The canals linking Barisal to Jhalokathi wind through countryside of rice paddies and guava orchards—the region produces thousands of tons of this fruit dubbed the 'Bengal apple', sold directly from boats.
Bhimruli floating market at Jhalokathi has operated for over a century: farmers and wholesalers trade goods from their boats, never setting foot on land.
Swarupkathi and its traditional shipbuilding workshops: craftsmen hand-shape wooden boats using techniques passed down through generations, without a single store-bought nail.

activities.text: Set off at dawn on a motorboat chartered for the day. The canal network around Barisal ranks among Bangladesh's densest—you'll pass schoolchildren in boats, fishermen casting nets and women washing laundry from their pontoons.

practical_info.tip: Charter a boat with guide from Barisal's main ghat

activities.text: Pass through a pottery village on the riverbank. Clay jars lined on the shore dry in the sun before firing—a tradition dating to Mughal times in this region.
In late afternoon, board the regular ferry to Hularhat to reach Khulna. The crossing follows the delta's branches at golden hour—the sky tinges orange over black water.

practical_info.transport: Ferry Barisal-Hularhat then road to Khulna

visits.text: Shat Gombuj Mosque (Sixty Domes) in Bagerhat, masterpiece of the Bengal Sultanate inscribed as UNESCO World Heritage in 1985: built between 1442 and 1459 by general-saint Khan Jahan Ali, governor of the Sundarbans. Despite its name, the building has 77 low brick domes supported by 60 stone pillars—medieval Bangladesh's largest mosque. Its 11 arches on the eastern façade, 2-meter-thick walls and undecorated terracotta brick facing reflect the unique 'Khan Jahan' style in Islamic architectural history. Archaeological museum on site.

Khan Jahan Ali Mausoleum facing the Shat Gombuj mosque: tomb of Bagerhat's founder (died 1459), an animated pilgrimage site. The brick mausoleum is surrounded by a pond inhabited by venerated crocodiles—a local tradition going back centuries.
Nine Dome Mosque (Noy Gombuj Masjid) in Bagerhat: a well-preserved edifice showcasing the terracotta brick floral decoration of Bengali style. Three rows of three domes on a square plan.

activities.text: Morning excursion to Bagerhat: the Sixty Dome mosque (Shait Gumbad) is the jewel of Sultan Khan Jahan Ali's 15th-century empire. Its brick vaults rest on 77 stone pillars—the interior acoustics are striking.
Evening cruise on the Rupsha River from Khulna. Fishing boats return laden with giant prawns—the city smells of iodine and spices, and warehouse lights reflect on black water.

visits.text: The Sundarbans, the world's largest mangrove forest (approximately 10,000 km², 6,000 km² on the Bangladesh side): classified UNESCO World Heritage for its exceptional natural value. The Ganges-Brahmaputra delta fragments here into hundreds of channels between islands covered with sundari mangrove trees. Habitat of the Bengal tiger (114 individuals estimated on the Bangladesh side), Irrawaddy dolphin, saltwater crocodile and 300 bird species.

Karamjal Eco-tourism Center in Mongla: the official entry point to Bangladesh's Sundarbans, with a care center for crocodiles and turtles, panoramic observation tower and walking trails along the forest edge. Spotted deer visible in large numbers. Boardwalks on stilts.

activities.text: Navigate in silent wooden boats from Khulna Jahaj Ghat or Mongla into the Sundarbans' secondary channels: early morning departure, 3-hour cruise through the mangrove. Bengal tigers, monitor lizards and Irrawaddy dolphins can be observed from the deck. Best season November-March.

practical_info.tip: Permit mandatory at Khulna Divisional Forest Office. Cholera vaccine recommended. Departing from Mongla saves 4-6h navigation.
practical_info.warning: Keep the armed forest guard within reach at all times inside the forest.

activities.text: Birdwatching in the creeks: Brown-winged Kingfisher, White-bellied Sea Eagle, Oriental Pied Hornbill, Masked Finfoot (critically endangered species). Binoculars essential.

to_next_leg.road_type: bus or train Khulna-Rajshahi via Jessore (~5-6h)

visits.text: Kotka, in the heart of the Sundarbans, is one of the few places to observe Bengal tigers from watchtowers. Forest guides point out fresh tracks in tidal mud—a paw print as wide as a dinner plate.

Harbaria Sanctuary shelters particularly dense mangrove. The Sundari mangroves (Heritiera fomes) that give the forest its name reach remarkable heights here—their aerial roots form labyrinths beneath which crocodiles glide.
At Karamjal Crocodile Breeding Center, hundreds of gharials are raised before release into the channels. Observation platforms overlook pools where reptiles pile atop each other.

activities.text: Navigate the Sundarbans channels by night, engine off. In total darkness, you perceive forest sounds—heavy breathing of an animal onshore, splash of a freshwater dolphin, shrill cry of a kingfisher.

practical_info.tip: Night excursion organized from houseboat, torches prohibited

activities.text: Trek through the mangrove at low tide with an armed forest guard. The buttress roots of Sundari trees create uneven, unstable ground—the forest silence is broken by the muffled sound of earth breathing.

practical_info.tip: Forest guard mandatory, stay in group

activities.text: Irrawaddy dolphin watching at sunrise on the Passur River. These freshwater cetaceans swim upstream early morning—guides spot their dorsal fins at water level.

visits.text: The mazar of Fakir Lalon Shah in Chheuria, 2 km from Kushtia station, is the heart of Baul philosophy. This 18th-century mystic rejected all religious boundaries between Hindus and Muslims—his songs still challenge orthodoxy.

Shilaidaha Kuthibari, 20 km from town in Kumarkhali, was Rabindranath Tagore's residence from 1890 to 1901. Here, facing the Padma, he wrote the poems translated into English as Gitanjali, which earned him the 1913 Nobel Prize.
Hardinge Bridge, 1.8 km long, has spanned the Padma since 1915. This masterpiece of British railway engineering—15 steel spans resting on masonry piers—is listed among Bangladesh's historic bridges.

activities.text: In evening, Lalon Shah's disciples gather in the akhara (meditation hall) around the mazar and chant baul songs to dotara and khamak. They've sung the same melodies for generations.

practical_info.tip: Gatherings are most intense during Lalon Mela (March and October)

activities.text: Stroll the Padma riverbanks at sunset from Shilaidaha. The Kuthibari terrace overlooks the river Tagore contemplated while composing poems—the view remains almost identical to what he immortalized in his writings.
Kumarkhali village produces artisanal silk (Kushtia silk) on traditional looms. Visit weavers directly to watch sari-making and purchase the finest pieces at their source.

visits.text: Puthia Temple Complex, 23 km east of Rajshahi: Bangladesh's largest assemblage of historic Hindu temples—14 classified structures erected by the Raj family of Puthia between the 17th and 19th centuries. The temples cluster around Lake Shyam Sagar. Terracotta architecture in Pancharatna, Jor-Bangla and Char-Chala styles, with façades covered in panels illustrating the Ramayana, Mahabharata and Radha-Krishna love stories.

Bhubaneshwar Shiva Temple in Puthia, Bangladesh's largest Shiva temple: built in 1823 by Rani Bhubonmoyee Devi in five-spire Pancharatna style. The central spire accumulates 108 sub-spires. Inside, a colossal black basalt lingam that the Pakistani army tried and failed to destroy in 1971.

Varendra Research Museum in Rajshahi, founded in 1910: one of the subcontinent's oldest museums. Collections of stone sculptures from the Pala kings' reign (9th-12th centuries), Buddhist and Hindu bronzes, Mughal coins and terracotta pieces. Remarkable Pala-period basalt carved heads.

activities.text: Drive from Kushtia to Rajshahi (~1h30 by car, 97 km). The West Bengal plain unfolds—rice paddies, mango orchards and red brick villages to the banks of the Padma.

visits.text: Mahasthangarh is Bangladesh's oldest urban archaeological site, dated to at least the 3rd century BCE. The brick ramparts of ancient Pundranagara still rise 6 meters above the river level.

Govinda Bhita temple contains remains of two superimposed Buddhist sanctuaries. Franco-Bangladeshi excavations uncovered 18 successive construction levels spanning over 1,500 years of uninterrupted occupation.

Mahasthangarh Museum displays a collection of terracotta plaques, silver Maurya coins, Brahmi inscriptions and Pala bronzes. The limestone plaque discovered in 1931, bearing six lines in Prakrit, is Bangladesh's oldest known written historical evidence.
Vasu Vihara, 3 km north of the citadel, is a large Buddhist monastery whose excavations reveal stupas, monks' cells and decorative elements from the Gupta-Pala period (4th-12th century).

activities.text: Walk the fortified perimeter of the citadel. The ramparts measuring 1.5 km long by 1.4 km wide once surrounded an entire town—tall grass and ongoing excavations lend the site an authentic exploration feel.

practical_info.tip: Local guide recommended to locate unmarked mounds

activities.text: Walk through Mahasthan village along the Karatoya. Inhabitants literally live above archaeological layers—some gardens still yield Maurya pottery shards on the surface.
In evening, Bogura town offers an animated night market where local confections—notably Bogura doi (thick fermented yogurt) renowned throughout Bangladesh—are served fresh.

visits.text: Star Mosque (Tara Masjid) in Old Dhaka is covered in blue faience mosaics inlaid with stars—Japanese artisans contributed to its decoration in the 19th century. One of Bangladesh's most photographed mosques.

Armenian Church of Saint Thomas, built in 1781, testifies to a prosperous Armenian merchant community in Dhaka during the 17th and 18th centuries. Its adjacent cemetery contains epitaphs of traders who came from Isfahan.

activities.text: Lunch break in Gulshan district after the journey from Bogura. The capital shows a modern face here—cafés, restaurants and shopping centers for a respite before the late afternoon departure to Sylhet.
Bus or overnight train Dhaka-Sylhet (4-5h). Parabat Express and Upaban Express connect Kamalapur to Sylhet several times daily—advance booking strongly advised.

practical_info.tip: Book Parabat Express or Upaban Express in advance

visits.text: The mazar of Hazrat Shah Jalal, 14th-century Sufi saint who Islamized the Sylhet region, welcomes thousands of pilgrims daily. Its turquoise domes and faience tiles gleam in morning sun. An adjacent pond is populated by catfish (gazar mach) that the faithful have fed for centuries—legend says they accompanied the saint.

Malnicherra Tea Estate, founded in 1854, is Bangladesh's oldest tea estate. Its rows of dome-trimmed bushes extend across thousands of hectares around Sylhet—sari-clad pluckers traverse the rows with baskets on their backs.
Osmani Museum honors General M.A.G. Osmani, commander-in-chief of Bangladesh liberation forces in 1971. Its collections of uniforms, battle maps and photographs document the independence war.
Ratargul Swamp Forest, 26 km from Sylhet, is one of the world's few freshwater flooded forests. In rainy season, Koroi trunks emerge from transparent emerald-green water.

activities.text: Navigate in a flat-bottomed boat through Ratargul Swamp Forest. Oars glide between submerged roots, kingfishers dive inches from the bow. It's one of Bangladesh's most singular experiences.

practical_info.tip: Rent a boat with oarsman from Goain Ghat. Best period: June-October (flooded forest)

activities.text: Evening stroll along the Surma riverbanks from Keane Bridge. The river divides Sylhet into two distinct banks—the Shah Jalal mosque bank and the more commercial Zindabazar bank—animated until late at night.
Zindabazar streets offer Sylhet's culinary specialties: coconut pitha (rice cake), Srimangal seven-layer tea if you haven't yet visited the plantations, and fish curry with Khasi herbs.

visits.text: Srimangal, Bangladesh's tea capital: the town is encircled by over 150 tea gardens spreading across gentle hills with rows of tea bushes trimmed to picking height. Finlay Tea Estate on the road to Lawachara is one of the most accessible. Sari-clad pluckers still hand-harvest two leaves and a bud at a time. Main plucking season runs from March to November.

Manipuri village of Srimangal: Bangladesh's Manipuri community is renowned for hand-woven textiles and traditional dances. The Manipuri craft market offers fabrics with distinctive geometric patterns and silver jewelry. A village visit lets you watch women working at domestic looms.

activities.text: Morning journey Sylhet → Srimangal by bus or CNG (~1h30, 55 km). The road crosses green hills and passes the first tea plantations from Srimangal's outskirts.

practical_info.transport: Direct bus Sylhet→Srimangal or shared CNG

activities.text: Taste the seven-layer tea at Nilkantha Tea Cabin—a local invention by Romesh Ram Gour, each layer using different tea (milk, ginger, cinnamon, lemon, mint...) without mixing.
Bicycle ride on rented bike through secondary roads between plantations in late afternoon.

visits.text: Lawachara National Park, a 1,250-hectare semi-evergreen tropical forest established as a British plantation program in 1922: 400 recorded species of plants, mammals and reptiles. Its most famous residents are western Hoolock gibbons—Bangladesh's only great apes, listed as critically endangered. The park also shelters capped langurs, pig-tailed macaques and mouse deer. Two Khasi villages live inside the forest.

Khasi village of Magurchara Punji inside Lawachara: 40 households from one of the world's rare matriarchal tribes. Bangladesh's Khasi people live by cultivating betel leaves and speak an Austro-Asiatic language distinct from Bengali. They are predominantly Christian in a 90% Muslim country.

Baikka Beel, a 100-hectare wetland sanctuary on the eastern edge of Hail Haor, 18 km from Srimangal: declared a fish reserve in 2003. In winter (November-February), thousands of migratory birds from Central Asia and Siberia land here—ducks, grebes, herons, kingfishers. Panoramic observation tower on stilts. Boat rides available.

activities.text: Guided trek through Lawachara in early morning—gibbons are active between 7am and 10am and their calls echo throughout the forest at sunrise.

practical_info.tip: Leave before 8am to maximize chances of observing gibbons.

activities.text: Boat ride on Baikka Beel in late afternoon—the low light on water lilies and silhouettes of herons in flight is one of Bangladesh's most peaceful images.

visits.text: Ethnological Museum of Agrabad in Chittagong: one of Bangladesh's rare ethnographic museums, devoted to the tribal peoples of Chittagong Hill Tracts—Chakma, Marma, Tripura, Khyang and Bawm. Collections of textiles, jewelry, musical instruments and everyday objects presented by ethnic group. Essential before heading to Rangamati.

Chandanpura Masjid in Chittagong's old commercial quarter: an 18th-century mosque with façades adorned with polychrome blue and white faience tiles—a decoration exceedingly rare in Bangladeshi religious architecture.
Patenga Sea Beach at the mouth of the Karnaphuli, facing the Bay of Bengal. View of cargo ships entering Chittagong port—Bangladesh's second export port. Popular promenade at sunset.

activities.text: Early train from Srimangal to Chittagong (~6h journey). Recommended departure around 6-7am to arrive early afternoon.

map_keywords: Cox's Bazar Sea Beach Longest World

visits.text: Cox's Bazar beach stretches 120 km uninterrupted—the world's longest unbroken natural beach. The Laboni segment at the town's heart is the epicenter of activity: strollers, coconut vendors and fishermen hauling nets onto sand.
Himchari National Park, 12 km south, blends tropical jungle, waterfalls and sea panoramas. From the hilltop—accessible by 200 steps—you watch the beach curve vanish into northern haze.

Aggmeda Khyang Monastery, founded centuries ago by the Rakhine community, preserves Buddhist manuscripts, bronze statues and murals depicting the Buddha's life. An enclave of serenity a few hundred meters from the beach tumult.

activities.text: Wake before dawn for sunrise on the beach. Cox's Bazar is one of the world's rare places where you simultaneously watch the sun rise over the sea and green hills blaze on the horizon.

The Burmese Market offers Rakhine crafts: traditional Longyi weavings, shell jewelry, local spices and dried fish. The vendors, descendants of the Rakhine community settled here for centuries, speak a dialect distinct from Bengali.

In late afternoon, walk or rickshaw along Marine Drive, the coastal road running several dozen kilometers along the beach. Sea to the right, forested hills to the left—the panorama is particularly striking at golden hour.

visits.text: Inani Beach, 33 km south of the center, is dotted with coral rocks and wave-sculpted limestone formations. Less crowded than Laboni, its waters are more transparent—you can make out the seabed several meters deep.

Ramu holds the region's largest concentration of Buddhist temples. The village, inhabited by the Rakhine community for centuries, centers on a huge gilded Buddha statue visible from the national highway.

Maheshkhali Island, accessible in 30 minutes by boat from Cox's Bazar, bears on its highest point the Adinath temple dedicated to Shiva—a major Hindu pilgrimage site in Bangladesh. The view over the delta and sea from the summit is unparalleled.

activities.text: Boat crossing to Maheshkhali (30 min from Cox's Bazar ghat). The island produces betel, salt and dried shrimp—its markets smell of iodine and spices. Fishermen mend nets on the shore while singing.

practical_info.tip: Early morning departure from Cox's Bazar ghat

activities.text: Snorkeling around Inani Beach's coral formations. Rocky breakers protect a calm zone where parrotfish and starfish frolic. Vendors rent masks and snorkels on the beach.

practical_info.tip: Best water visibility in dry season (November-April)

activities.text: Sunset on Inani Beach from the flat coral rocks. Low light transforms tide pools into golden mirrors—a panorama photographers come to capture from across South Asia.

visits.text: Kaptai Lake, Bangladesh's largest artificial lake, created in 1960 for a hydroelectric dam on the Karnaphuli: its 777 km² of emerald water stretch between hills covered with dense forests dotted with islands and Chakma stilt villages. By boat, you pass fishermen hauling nets, women washing laundry on shores and buffaloes bathing in coves.

Rangamati Hanging Bridge (Jhulonto Bridge), built in 1984 by Bangladesh Parjatan Corporation on Kaptai Lake shores: 335 feet of red metal walkway connecting two hills above the lake. An iconic Rangamati symbol. Warning: the bridge is submerged in full rainy season (August-September) when lake levels rise.

Rajban Vihar, Bangladesh's largest Buddhist temple on Kaptai Lake shores in Rangamati: a wooden and concrete complex sheltering a large gilded Buddha statue. Spiritual center of the predominantly Theravada Buddhist Chakma community. Peaceful atmosphere, direct lake view.

activities.text: Drive Chittagong → Rangamati in morning (77 km, ~2h on winding hill road). The journey itself is spectacular: hairpin turns on wooded ridges with plunging valley views.

Boat cruise chartered at Reserve Bazar ghat to Shuvolong Waterfall (~1h30 one-way): crossing the main lake, passing between banana-covered islands and arriving in a canyon with steep walls where the waterfall tumbles.

practical_info.tip: Negotiate boat price at Reserve Bazar ghat. Plan for full day.

map_keywords: Buddha Dhatu Jadi Golden Temple Bandarban

visits.text: Buddha Dhatu Jadi—the Golden Temple—is Bangladesh's largest Theravada Buddhist temple. Perched on a 60-meter hill 4 km from the center, it houses a Buddha relic gifted by Myanmar and the country's second-largest Buddha statue.

Nilgiri, 46 km south of town, rises to 1,066 meters—one of Bangladesh's highest points. Clouds roll beneath the ridge in morning. The slopes are home to the Mro, one of the Chittagong Hill Tracts' most ancient tribal peoples.
Chimbuk Hill, Bangladesh's third-highest summit at 762 meters, is reached by a winding road carved through tropical forest. Marma villages cling to slopes—their stilt bamboo houses defy the gradient.

activities.text: Morning trek on ridges around Nilgiri with a local Mro guide. Trails cross giant bamboo forests and terraced rice cultivation—at a bend, you glimpse the Chittagong plain 1,000 meters below.

practical_info.tip: Guide mandatory, trekking permit required at army checkpoint

activities.text: Descend by Chander Gari jeep on the Chimbuk road, windows open to the valleys. These open vehicles are the traditional hill transport—locals pile onto wooden benches singing.

practical_info.transport: Chander Gari (local jeep) chartered for the day from Bandarban Sadar

activities.text: Cruise the Sangu River at sunset from Bandarban Sadar. The river rises in Burma and crosses spectacular gorges before reaching the plains—Marma fishermen cast their circular nets from frail dugouts.

visits.text: Patenga Beach at the mouth of the Karnaphuli offers views of giant container ships entering Chittagong port—Bangladesh's most important, handling 90% of the country's foreign trade.

Chittagong's Ethnological Museum is Bangladesh's only one of its kind. Its collections document the lifestyles, costumes, tools and beliefs of the country's 27 tribal communities, including the Chakma, Marma and Mro recently visited in Bandarban.

activities.text: Night stroll through Sadarghat district along the Karnaphuli. Vessels of all sizes navigate under illuminated bridges—a final maritime image before returning to Dhaka.
Foys Lake, 8 km from the center, is an artificial lake framed by forested hills. In evening, Chittagong residents gather to stroll and dine at stilt restaurants.

visits.text: Jatiya Sangsad Bhaban, Dhaka's National Parliament, masterwork by American architect Louis I. Kahn designed between 1962 and 1982: one of the 20th century's most important buildings according to architectural history. Nine geometric concrete blocks—circles, triangles and hollowed-out trapezoids—articulate around a grand central hall. Striking exterior view from the artificial lake shores.

Kawran Bazar market, Dhaka's largest wholesale vegetable and fish market: active from 4am, a striking spectacle of the food logistics for a 22-million-person megacity. Mounds of red chilies, boatloads of Ganges catfish, towers of yellow melons.

Liberation War Museum in central Dhaka: opened in 1996, it documents the 1971 genocide perpetrated by the Pakistani army. Photographs, testimonies and personal objects constitute a sober, moving account of nine months of war. Essential for understanding the Bangladeshi nation.

activities.text: Train or bus from Chittagong to Dhaka in morning (~5-6h). Subarna Express and Turna Nishita connect Chittagong to Dhaka several times daily from Chittagong station.

practical_info.transport: Train Subarna Express or Turna Nishita, Chittagong→Dhaka, ~5-6h

activities.text: Final evening in Old Dhaka: dinner at a Nawabpur or Bangshal street restaurant specializing in Bengali cuisine—goat biryani, mustard hilsa or Sylhet shatkora.

visits.text: Bangladesh National Museum at Shahbag: opened in 1913, it brings together 83,000 objects on four floors—ancient sculpture and architecture, Islamic art, ethnology and natural history. Pala sculpture gallery and jamdani textile collections are particularly remarkable.

activities.text: Free day before return flight. Shopping at New Market or Bashundhara City—jamdani fabrics, nakshi kantha (embroidery), bamboo baskets and spices.
Transfer to Hazrat Shahjalal International Airport (DAC). Allow 3h before international departure for formalities.

seo.h1_title: 22-Day Bangladesh Itinerary: Delta, Sundarbans, Sylhet, Cox's Bazar & Bandarban
seo.intro_paragraph: From frenetic Dhaka to the Sundarbans mangroves where tigers prowl, from Sylhet's tea plantations to Cox's Bazar beaches and Bandarban's tribal peaks, this 22-day circuit traverses Bangladesh in all its diversity: rivers, forests, hills and coasts.
seo.seo_hook: Dugouts glide through the mangrove darkness. Further north, baul songs rise from a mystical mazar. On the world's longest beach, evening light gilds coral rocks. Bangladesh reveals its faces one after another, to those who take the time to cross it.
featured_list: Barisal: Venice of Bengal and floating guava markets
Sundarbans: two nights in Bengal tiger kingdom
Kushtia: mystical pilgrimage in the footsteps of Lalon Shah and Tagore
Cox's Bazar: 120 km of beach, the world's longest
Bandarban: tribal hills and golden Buddhist temple

places_taglines.BD::dhaka: Where the thunder of ten thousand rickshaw bells drowns out everything else
places_taglines.BD::barisal: The Venice of Bengal, where boats outnumber cars ten to one
places_taglines.BD::khulna: Gateway to the Sundarbans and sentinel of the southwestern delta
places_taglines.BD::sundarbans: Where the ocean breathes through mangrove roots and tigers leave paw prints in the mud
places_taglines.BD::kushtia: Where Baul mystics still sing songs that dissolve the boundaries between faiths
places_taglines.BD::bogura: Guarding the ruins of Pundranagara, Bangladesh's most ancient city
places_taglines.BD::sylhet: Where emerald tea gardens ripple across hills shrouded in mist
places_taglines.BD::srimangal: The kingdom of seven-layer tea and hoolock gibbon calls at dawn
places_taglines.BD::cox-bazar: Where the world's longest beach meets the Bay of Bengal in an endless embrace
places_taglines.BD::chattogram: Where the Karnaphuli carries cargo ships past Rakhine temples and colonial warehouses
places_taglines.BD::rangamati: Where Kaptai Lake fragments into a thousand islands crowned with Chakma stilt villages
places_taglines.BD::bandarban: Where clouds spill down hillsides and the Golden Temple catches the first light

highlights: PS Mahsud: overnight crossing by century-old paddle steamer Dhaka-Barisal
Sundarbans: two nights on houseboat in Bengal tiger mangroves
Cox's Bazar: 120 km of beach, the world's longest natural beach
Mahasthangarh: ancient city of Pundranagara dated to 3rd century BCE
Bandarban: Nilgiri and its clouds at 1,066 meters in Mro territory
warnings: Chittagong Hill Tracts (Rangamati, Bandarban): foreign nationals must obtain a permit from the Tribal Affairs Office in Dhaka or Chittagong before visiting the region.
D11 Dhaka transit: busy day (~8-9h cumulative road/train). Departure from Bogura recommended before 9am.
Sundarbans: licensed forest guide mandatory. Houseboat tours must be booked in advance from Khulna or Mongla.
PS Mahsud: check BIWTC schedule before travel—service operates Fridays only departing Dhaka.
